practices: The AP Calculus AB and BC courses assess four key Mathematical Practices. Practice 1: Implementing Mathematical Processes focuses on determining expressions and values using mathematical procedures and rules. Practice 2: Connecting Representations involves translating mathematical information between single or multiple representations (graphical, numerical, analytical, verbal). Practice 3: Justification requires students to justify reasoning and solutions using definitions, theorems, and tests. Practice 4: Communication and Notation emphasizes using correct notation, language, and mathematical conventions to communicate results. On the multiple-choice section, the weightings are Practice 1 (53–66%), Practice 2 (18–28%), and Practice 3 (11–18%); Practice 4 is not assessed. On the free-response section for both AB and BC exams, the weightings are Practice 1 (37–59%), Practice 2 (9–16%), Practice 3 (37–59%), and Practice 4 (9-24% for AB, 9-20% for BC).

examFormat.multipleChoice: 45 questions (Part A: 30 questions, 60 minutes, no calculator; Part B: 15 questions, 45 minutes, graphing calculator required), 50% of total score
examFormat.freeResponse: 6 questions (Part A: 2 questions, 30 minutes, graphing calculator required; Part B: 4 questions, 60 minutes, no calculator), 50% of total score

antiPatterns: The CED explicitly states that certain topics are not assessed. These include: the epsilon-delta definition of a limit; indeterminate forms other than 0/0 and ∞/∞ (such as ∞-∞, 1^∞, 0*∞); and series convergence tests beyond the nth Term Test, Integral Test, Comparison Tests, Alternating Series Test, and Ratio Test. The course de-emphasizes rote memorization of formulas and procedures, especially for series, in favor of a conceptual understanding of the limiting processes that connect discrete approximations to analytic calculations.
